albums.title: Kid A
albums.reason: With 2050 all-time plays Radiohead are deep in your bones, and Kid A sits at the cold, electronic heart of their catalogue — a record that rewards revisiting when you want something cerebral and immersive.

albums.title: Random Access Memories
albums.reason: Your top-played artist with tracks like Digital Love and One More Time in your all-time top 20 — RAM is the lush, human side of Daft Punk that deserves a full sit-down listen rather than shuffle play.

albums.title: Animals
albums.reason: With 1199 all-time Pink Floyd plays and The Dark Side of the Moon and Wish You Were Here already in your recent pool, Animals is the overlooked, razor-edged sibling that you probably haven't returned to recently.

albums.title: Physical Graffiti
albums.reason: Led Zeppelin sit at 964 all-time plays and both When the Levee Breaks and The Rain Song have already been surfaced as songs — Physical Graffiti is the double-album vault piece that goes far deeper than the classics.

albums.title: The Understanding
albums.reason: Röyksopp clock 1062 all-time plays and Melody A.M. has already been recommended — The Understanding is their most emotionally complex album and a worthy revisit from the vault.

albums.title: Beaucoup Fish
albums.reason: Born Slippy is in your all-time top 20 and Cowgirl was a recent song pick — Beaucoup Fish is the album that shows Underworld at their most euphoric and expansive, and it's one you've probably not played front-to-back in a while.

albums.title: Origin of Symmetry
albums.reason: Muse sit at 712 all-time plays, and Origin of Symmetry is their most ferocious and lasting record — a pure comfort-food vault pull when you want something that hits hard.

albums.title: Is This It
albums.reason: The Strokes are in your all-time top 20 at 652 plays — Is This It is one of those records where every single track earns its place, and it probably hasn't had a full spin from you in some time.

albums.title: II
albums.reason: Your love of Daft Punk, Justice and Leftfield points squarely at high-craft electronic albums with emotional depth — Moderat's II sits in exactly that space, melding Berlin techno architecture with something genuinely melancholic.

albums.title: Kill for Love
albums.reason: The atmospheric, after-midnight side of your all-time taste — Air, James Blake, Röyksopp — finds a natural companion in Chromatics' expansive, cinematic Kill for Love, a cult classic you've likely walked past.

albums.title: Maim That Tune
albums.reason: Your deep plays of Leftfield and Orbital in the mid-90s electronic space point right at Fila Brazillia — a criminally overlooked Sheffield duo making warm, sample-rich downtempo that sits in exactly that lane.

albums.title: Bricolage
albums.reason: With Orbital, Leftfield and Daft Punk so deep in your all-time list, Amon Tobin's Bricolage — intricate, jazz-infused drum and bass built from found sound — is the kind of cult record that should have found you years ago.

albums.title: Mezzanine
albums.reason: Radiohead's tension, Leftfield's density and James Blake's emotional weight all converge on Massive Attack's Mezzanine — a defining record of brooding, bass-heavy atmosphere that feels built for your all-time taste.

albums.title: Play
albums.reason: Your all-time palette of Daft Punk, Röyksopp and Air has a quiet soulful undertow — Moby's Play captures that perfectly, stitching blues and gospel vocals into immaculate electronic production in a way that still holds up completely.

albums.title: Fahrenheit Fair Enough
albums.reason: James Blake's emotional restraint and Radiohead's textural experimentation both point toward Telefon Tel Aviv's debut — a deeply moving, underheard record of glitchy IDM that carries genuine warmth and vulnerability.

albums.title: Every Day
albums.reason: The Killers' melodic ambition, Röyksopp's emotional sweep and Orbital's cinematic scope all point toward The Cinematic Orchestra — Every Day is their most fully realised album, alive with jazz, soul and aching beauty.

albums.title: Simple Things
albums.reason: Air's Moon Safari is deep in your all-time DNA, and Zero 7's Simple Things inhabits the same sunlit, slightly melancholic chillout space — a record full of vocal hooks and gentle electronic warmth that belongs in your collection.

albums.title: FLOTUS
albums.reason: Your recent arc through Bill Withers' soul warmth and Nils Frahm's intimate piano points at Lambchop's FLOTUS — an unexpected, deeply moving blend of Nashville soul and electronic texture that rewards slow, attentive listening.

albums.title: Persona
albums.reason: Your recent plays of Max Cooper, Weval and Apparat map closely onto Rival Consoles' Persona — a beautifully constructed album of modular, emotionally charged electronic music built for focused listening.

albums.title: Éthiopiques Vol. 21
albums.reason: Your recent deep immersion in solo piano — Felix Rösch, Hania Rani, Nils Frahm — makes Emahoy Tsegué-Maryam Guèbrou an essential discovery: her melancholy, prayer-like compositions are unlike anything else you'll find in this space.

albums.title: Pocket Symphonies
albums.reason: With Ólafur Arnalds, Nils Frahm and Ben Lukas Boysen all in heavy rotation, Sven Helbig's Pocket Symphonies — delicate, chamber-scaled compositions with electronic underpinning — feels like a natural and overdue discovery.

albums.title: Safe in the Hands of Love
albums.reason: Your recent Sault and Charles Bradley listening shows you're drawn to soul music with an experimental edge — Yves Tumor's Safe in the Hands of Love pushes that into genuinely strange, beautiful territory worth exploring.

albums.title: Forgotten Stars
albums.reason: Your recent listening to Brambles and Ólafur Arnalds points directly at this collaboration — cello, processed field recordings and piano building something quietly devastating that sits right alongside Charcoal in your current headspace.

albums.title: Liquid Spirit
albums.reason: Bill Withers' warmth and the Oscar Peterson Trio's late-night jazz are both deep in your recent listening — Gregory Porter's Liquid Spirit is the record that bridges those worlds with a voice and emotional presence that will stop you in your tracks.

albums.title: Harmless Feelings
albums.reason: Your recent Tosca, Super Flu and Dominik Eulberg plays show a clear appetite for organic, warm-textured house — Tornado Wallace's Harmless Feelings is a cult gem in exactly that vein, rooted in nature and genuine dancefloor feeling.

albums.title: All Melody
albums.reason: You've been playing Nils Frahm constantly this week — Circling, Harm Hymn and Fundamental Values are all from this album, so a full front-to-back listen of All Melody feels like exactly where you are right now.

albums.title: Solan
albums.reason: Erland Cooper has shown up in your last 7 days via the Holm reworks and you're clearly in a windswept, contemplative zone — Solan is his most recent solo record and carries the same elemental, place-rooted beauty you've been seeking.

albums.title: Some Kind of Peace
albums.reason: With Ólafur Arnalds appearing in your last 7 days and recent tracks like 21:05 and Love and Glory in heavy rotation, Some Kind of Peace is his most personal album and a perfect match for the quiet, introspective space you're inhabiting.

albums.title: Solipsism
albums.reason: Joep Beving has been a consistent presence this past month and Liminal has already been in your recent pool — Solipsism is his debut and the record that started it all, unhurried and deeply meditative in a way that fits your current week exactly.

albums.artist: A Winged Victory for the Sullen
albums.title: Invisible Cities
albums.reason: A Winged Victory for the Sullen are all over your last 7 days — So That The City Can Begin To Exist just played — and Invisible Cities is their most narrative, immersive work, written for the stage and built to be heard in one long sitting.

albums.title: Spells
albums.reason: Ben Lukas Boysen has been your second most-played artist this past month — Gravity is already in your recent pool, and Spells is the record where his neoclassical and electronic instincts balance most beautifully.

albums.title: Boom
albums.reason: Tosca are your most-played artist this past month and Suzuki has been on repeat — Boom reaches deeper into their dub and jazz influences and is one of their most cohesive and underplayed albums, ready for a proper discovery.

albums.title: Archipelago
albums.reason: Hidden Orchestra just appeared in your last 7 days and the East London Street track shows you're drawn to their intricate, natural-world construction — Archipelago is their most fully realised work and a record to get properly lost in.

songs.title: How to Disappear Completely
songs.reason: Reckoner is your most-played Radiohead track at 125 plays, which shows you return to their most emotionally open moments — How to Disappear Completely is the Kid A centrepiece that reaches those same depths and deserves to be back in rotation.

songs.title: Shine On You Crazy Diamond (Pts. 1-5)
songs.reason: With 1199 all-time Pink Floyd plays and two albums already surfaced, this nine-minute opener from Wish You Were Here is a vault piece that earns its own dedicated listen — slow, devastating and utterly irreplaceable.

songs.title: A Day in the Life
songs.reason: The Beatles sit at 831 all-time plays in your catalogue and A Day in the Life is the peak they never surpassed — an all-time track that you probably haven't played intentionally in years and that still sounds like nothing else.

songs.title: Melt
songs.reason: Release the Pressure is in your all-time top 20 at 120 plays and Leftism has already been recommended — Melt from Rhythm and Stealth is one of the most seductive tracks they ever made and a worthy vault retrieval.

songs.title: Remind
songs.reason: Orbital sit at 777 all-time plays and both Halcyon + On + On and the Brown Album have already been surfaced — Remind, from Snivilisation, is the track that captures their more melancholic, searching side and deserves to come back out of the vault.

songs.title: Human Nature
songs.reason: With 1852 all-time Michael Jackson plays you clearly love him deeply — Human Nature from Thriller is the most underrated track on that album, late-night and wistful in a way that will hit differently coming back to it now.

Arctic Monkeys sit at 809 all-time plays, and 505 from Favourite Worst Nightmare is one of their most enduring, emotionally charged moments — a vault track that grows more powerful every time you return to it.

songs.title: Runaway
songs.reason: Kanye appears in your all-time top 20 at 623 plays — Runaway from My Beautiful Dark Twisted Fantasy is the centrepiece of his most ambitious work and the kind of nine-minute statement that demands a proper, undistracted listen.

songs.title: Two Dots
songs.reason: Your all-time depth in Orbital, Leftfield and Underworld — precision electronic music with emotional undertow — maps perfectly onto Lusine's Two Dots, a track that floats between IDM and ambient house with effortless beauty.

songs.title: Eyen
songs.reason: The Orbital and Daft Punk quadrant of your all-time taste — melodic, detailed, electronic — makes Plaid essential listening, and Eyen is their most immediately arresting recent track: intricate, warm and slightly bittersweet.

songs.title: N.E.W.
songs.reason: The colder, more experimental edge of your all-time taste — Radiohead's Kid A, Leftfield's density — finds its logical underground extension in Actress, and N.E.W. is one of the most distinctive tracks in his catalogue.

songs.title: Toneblind
songs.reason: The sweeping, textural dimension of your all-time Radiohead and Pink Floyd listening suggests you'd respond to the stark, quietly vast world of this collaboration — a hidden gem in minimalist composition that deserves your ears.

songs.title: Black Water
songs.reason: James Blake's fragile emotionalism and Radiohead's gothic weight both point toward this rare collaboration — Black Water is a startling, deeply atmospheric track that lives in the darkest corner of that Venn diagram.

songs.title: All Dressed Up and Smelling of Strangers
songs.reason: Your all-time appetite for high-craft electronic music with real compositional ambition — Daft Punk, Orbital, Justice — makes The Flashbulb a natural discovery, and this track is his most emotionally striking work.

songs.title: Monument
songs.reason: With Röyksopp at 1062 all-time plays and their emotional electronic style clearly beloved, this collaboration from the Do It Again EP is a nine-minute piece of devastating synth-pop grandeur that should have been on your radar long ago.

songs.title: Seraph
songs.reason: Your all-time love of James Blake, Röyksopp and the atmospheric edge of your electronic taste points directly at Submotion Orchestra — Seraph is their most luminous track, orchestral and bass-heavy in equal measure.

songs.title: Strobe
songs.reason: Your all-time depth in Daft Punk's epic, slow-building electronic tracks — Veridis Quo, Nightvision, Superheroes — means Strobe is the natural adjacent discovery: ten minutes of pure, aching ascent that operates on the same emotional frequency.

songs.title: Lapis
songs.reason: Your recent Oscar Peterson Trio and 9 Lazy 9 listening points toward jazz with depth and warmth, and Tenderlonious's Lapis is a beautiful, flute-led spiritual jazz piece that bridges your jazz listening and your current meditative mood.

songs.title: Hypnosis
songs.reason: Your recent Oscar Peterson Trio plays and the soul-jazz thread running through your current listening make Kamaal Williams a natural fit — Hypnosis is a hypnotic, locked-groove piece of modern jazz that flows like water.

songs.title: Space 1.8
songs.reason: Your recent Ólafur Arnalds and Hania Rani plays alongside the Oscar Peterson Trio suggest you'd love where ambient and jazz dissolve into each other — Space 1.8 is a 14-minute drift that does exactly that with stunning grace.

songs.title: Warm
songs.reason: Your recent Bill Withers soul warmth and the organic, warm-textured electronics of Tosca and 9 Lazy 9 both point toward Catching Flies — Warm is a sleepy, gorgeous track that sits right in that intersection.

songs.title: ESP
songs.reason: Your recent arc through Max Cooper, Weval and Apparat combined with the Oscar Peterson Trio's jazz presence makes Portico's ESP a perfect discovery — moody, electronic-tinged jazz built for your current late-night headspace.

songs.title: I Will Believe
songs.reason: Your recent Sault and Charles Bradley listening — soulful, purposeful music — alongside your jazz thread with the Oscar Peterson Trio points toward Alabaster dePlume, whose I Will Believe is a quiet, deeply moving piece of spiritual beauty.

songs.title: On the Nature of Daylight
songs.reason: Your recent Brambles, Ólafur Arnalds and Hania Rani listening paints a clear picture of someone who loves slow, emotionally devastating string-led music — Max Richter's On the Nature of Daylight is the canonical piece in that space you may not have found yet.

songs.title: Dance
songs.reason: Your Sault and 9 Lazy 9 listening shows genuine appetite for soul and R&B approached unconventionally — Young Jesus's Dance is a 10-minute piece of warm, exploratory post-rock-adjacent soul that rewards patient, undivided attention.

songs.title: Says
songs.reason: Says is already in your recent top tracks at 7 plays this year and you've been deep in Nils Frahm this week — it's worth putting on with full attention as a standalone piece rather than letting it arrive on shuffle.

songs.artist: A Winged Victory for the Sullen
songs.title: Ademollo
songs.reason: You've been playing A Winged Victory for the Sullen across multiple albums this week and this track from their debut is one of their most quietly overwhelming moments — a perfect companion to the Steep Hills and Requiem tracks you've had on.

songs.title: Near Light
songs.reason: With Ólafur Arnalds firmly in your last 7 days and tracks like 21:05 and Love and Glory just played, Near Light from And They Have Escaped is the track that distills everything you're responding to in him right now into four perfect minutes.

songs.title: Nocturne
songs.reason: Ben Lukas Boysen has been your second most-played artist this month — Nocturne from the Dreamlike album is one of his most intimate solo piano pieces and fits perfectly into the late-night, introspective space you've been inhabiting this week.

songs.title: Chocolate Elvis
songs.reason: Tosca are your most-played artist this month and you've been deep in Suzuki — Chocolate Elvis from their debut of the same name is one of their most beloved and sensuous tracks, perfect for where your head is right now.

songs.title: Hether Blether
songs.reason: Erland Cooper appeared in your last 7 days via the Holm reworks and you're clearly responding to his sound right now — Hether Blether from Solan is a beautifully open, sea-breezed track that matches the contemplative mood of your recent listening perfectly.

songs.title: Woven
songs.reason: Joep Beving has been a consistent presence in your past month and Liminal is already in your pool — Woven from Hermetism is a slow, deeply meditative solo piano piece that fits the introspective, quiet zone you've been in this week.

songs.title: Kittiwake
songs.reason: Martyn Heyne appeared in your last 7 days with Fårö and Electric Intervals was recently in your pool — Kittiwake from Intervals is a quietly stunning guitar piece that matches the unhurried, open-sky feeling of everything you've been playing this week.

djSets.setName: HMWL Podcast 430: Niconé & David Hasert (Kompakt / Get Physical / HMWL)
Daphni - FOLD, London. Nov 22, 2025. 7hr DJ set
Klausmarie b2b Alec Troniq - RAVIN' RAVIN' 2026 @ Sektor Evolution
